examples.title: Algorithm: Chain Operations in Pseudocode
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating the fundamental operations on a chain: node structure, creation, insertion, traversal, and deletion
examples.code:
ALGORITHM DefineNodeStructure
// Defines a self-referential node structure
OUTPUT: Node structure with data and next pointer fields

BEGIN
 STRUCT Node
 data : Integer
 next : Pointer to Node
 END STRUCT
END

ALGORITHM CreateNode(value)
// Allocates memory for a new node and initializes it
INPUT: value - data to be stored in the node
OUTPUT: Pointer to the newly created node

BEGIN
 newNode ← ALLOCATE(sizeof(Node))
 IF newNode = NULL THEN
 PRINT "Memory allocation failed"
 EXIT PROGRAM
 END IF
 newNode.data ← value
 newNode.next ← NULL
 RETURN newNode
END

ALGORITHM InsertAtBeginning(head, value)
// Inserts a new node at the beginning of the chain
INPUT: head - pointer to first node, value - data for new node
OUTPUT: Pointer to new head node

BEGIN
 newNode ← CreateNode(value)
 newNode.next ← head
 RETURN newNode
END

ALGORITHM TraverseAndDisplay(head)
// Visits each node and prints its data
INPUT: head - pointer to first node
OUTPUT: Displays all node values

BEGIN
 current ← head
 WHILE current ≠ NULL DO
 PRINT current.data, " -> "
 current ← current.next
 END WHILE
 PRINT "NULL"
END

ALGORITHM CountNodes(head)
// Counts the number of nodes in the chain
INPUT: head - pointer to first node
OUTPUT: Integer count of nodes

BEGIN
 count ← 0
 current ← head
 WHILE current ≠ NULL DO
 count ← count + 1
 current ← current.next
 END WHILE
 RETURN count
END

ALGORITHM SearchNode(head, key)
// Searches for a value in the chain
INPUT: head - pointer to first node, key - value to search
OUTPUT: Pointer to node if found, NULL otherwise

BEGIN
 current ← head
 WHILE current ≠ NULL DO
 IF current.data = key THEN
 RETURN current
 END IF
 current ← current.next
 END WHILE
 RETURN NULL
END

ALGORITHM DeleteNode(head, key)
// Deletes the first node containing the key value
INPUT: head - pointer to first node, key - value to delete
OUTPUT: Updated head pointer

BEGIN
 IF head = NULL THEN
 RETURN NULL
 END IF
 
 // If head node contains the key
 IF head.data = key THEN
 temp ← head
 head ← head.next
 DEALLOCATE temp
 RETURN head
 END IF
 
 // Search for the node to delete
 current ← head
 WHILE current.next ≠ NULL AND current.next.data ≠ key DO
 current ← current.next
 END WHILE
 
 IF current.next ≠ NULL THEN
 temp ← current.next
 current.next ← temp.next
 DEALLOCATE temp
 END IF
 
 RETURN head
END

ALGORITHM ReverseChain(head)
// Reverses the chain direction
INPUT: head - pointer to first node
OUTPUT: Pointer to new head (formerly last node)

BEGIN
 prev ← NULL
 current ← head
 WHILE current ≠ NULL DO
 next ← current.next // Save next node
 current.next ← prev // Reverse the link
 prev ← current // Move prev forward
 current ← next // Move current forward
 END WHILE
 RETURN prev
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates all fundamental chain operations: defining the self-referential structure, creating nodes with dynamic memory allocation, inserting at the beginning, traversing to display and count nodes, searching for values, deleting nodes, and reversing the chain. The traversal pattern (starting at head, moving through next until NULL) is the foundation for most chain algorithms.

examples.title: Python: Chain (Linked List) Implementation
examples.description: Python implementation demonstrating linked list concepts with classes and dynamic memory management
examples.code:
class Node:
 """
 Self-referential structure for a chain node.
 Each node contains data and a reference to the next node.
 """
 def __init__(self, data):
 self.data = data
 self.next = None # Reference to next node (None = end of chain)
 
 def __repr__(self):
 return f"Node({self.data})"


class Chain:
 """
 Singly Linked List implementation with basic operations.
 The 'head' attribute points to the first node.
 """
 def __init__(self):
 self.head = None # Empty chain starts with head = None
 
 def is_empty(self):
 """Check if the chain is empty."""
 return self.head is None
 
 def create_node(self, value):
 """
 Create a new node (analogous to malloc in C).
 Returns the new node object.
 """
 new_node = Node(value)
 return new_node
 
 def insert_at_beginning(self, value):
 """
 Insert a new node at the beginning of the chain.
 Time Complexity: O(1)
 """
 new_node = self.create_node(value)
 new_node.next = self.head # Link new node to current head
 self.head = new_node # Update head to new node
 
 def insert_at_end(self, value):
 """
 Insert a new node at the end of the chain.
 Time Complexity: O(n)
 """
 new_node = self.create_node(value)
 
 if self.head is None:
 # Empty chain - new node becomes head
 self.head = new_node
 return
 
 # Traverse to the last node
 current = self.head
 while current.next is not None:
 current = current.next
 
 # Link last node to new node
 current.next = new_node
 
 def display(self):
 """
 Traverse and display all nodes in the chain.
 Pattern: Start at head, process, move to next until None.
 """
 if self.is_empty():
 print("Chain is empty")
 return
 
 nodes = []
 current = self.head
 
 while current is not None:
 nodes.append(str(current.data))
 current = current.next
 
 print(" -> ".join(nodes) + " -> NULL")
 
 def count_nodes(self):
 """
 Count total number of nodes in the chain.
 Time Complexity: O(n)
 """
 count = 0
 current = self.head
 
 while current is not None:
 count += 1
 current = current.next
 
 return count
 
 def search(self, key):
 """
 Search for a value in the chain.
 Returns True if found, False otherwise.
 Time Complexity: O(n)
 """
 current = self.head
 
 while current is not None:
 if current.data == key:
 return True
 current = current.next
 
 return False
 
 def delete_by_value(self, key):
 """
 Delete the first node with the given value.
 Time Complexity: O(n)
 """
 # Case 1: Empty chain
 if self.head is None:
 print("Chain is empty, cannot delete")
 return
 
 # Case 2: Head node contains the value
 if self.head.data == key:
 self.head = self.head.next
 print(f"Deleted {key} from beginning")
 return
 
 # Case 3: Search for node to delete
 current = self.head
 while current.next is not None and current.next.data != key:
 current = current.next
 
 # Node found - delete it
 if current.next is not None:
 current.next = current.next.next
 print(f"Deleted {key}")
 else:
 print(f"Value {key} not found in chain")
 
 def reverse(self):
 """
 Reverse the chain in place.
 Uses three-pointer technique (prev, current, next).
 Time Complexity: O(n), Space Complexity: O(1)
 """
 prev = None
 current = self.head
 
 while current is not None:
 next_temp = current.next # Save next node
 current.next = prev # Reverse the link
 prev = current # Move prev forward
 current = next_temp # Move current forward
 
 self.head = prev
 
 def free_chain(self):
 """
 Free all nodes to prevent memory leaks.
 In Python, this is handled by garbage collection.
 """
 self.head = None


# Demonstration of chain operations
if __name__ == "__main__":
 # Create a new chain
 chain = Chain()
 
 print("=== Chain Operations Demo ===\n")
 
 # Insert elements
 print("Inserting elements at beginning:")
 chain.insert_at_beginning(30)
 chain.insert_at_beginning(20)
 chain.insert_at_beginning(10)
 chain.display() # Output: 10 -> 20 -> 30 -> NULL
 
 # Insert at end
 print("\nInserting 40 at end:")
 chain.insert_at_end(40)
 chain.display() # Output: 10 -> 20 -> 30 -> 40 -> NULL
 
 # Count nodes
 print(f"\nNode count: {chain.count_nodes()}")
 
 # Search
 print(f"\nSearch for 25: {chain.search(25)}")
 print(f"Search for 30: {chain.search(30)}")
 
 # Delete
 print("\nDeleting 20:")
 chain.delete_by_value(20)
 chain.display()
 
 # Reverse
 print("\nReversing chain:")
 chain.reverse()
 chain.display()
examples.explanation: This Python implementation demonstrates all the key concepts from the C topic using Python's object-oriented approach. The Node class acts as a self-referential structure, the Chain class manages the head pointer, and operations like insert, delete, search, and reverse follow the same algorithmic patterns as in C. The display method shows the traversal pattern (start at head, process, move to next until None). Time complexities are identical to C implementations.
examples.timeComplexity: Insert O(1) at beginning, O(n) at end; Search O(n); Reverse O(n)
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for storage, O(1) for operations

examples.title: C: Chain (Singly Linked List) Implementation
examples.description: Complete C implementation demonstrating self-referential structures, dynamic memory allocation with malloc, and fundamental chain operations
examples.explanation: This C implementation is the core of the topic 'Representing Chains in C'. It demonstrates: (1) Self-referential structure with typedef, (2) createNode function with malloc and NULL checking, (3) Head pointer management, (4) Traversal pattern (while current != NULL), (5) Insert at beginning/end, (6) Delete by value with memory management using free(), and (7) Free chain to prevent memory leaks. The code follows university exam conventions and includes comprehensive comments explaining each step. The arrow operator (->) is used to access structure members through pointers.
examples.timeComplexity: Insert O(1) at beginning, O(n) at end; Delete O(n); Search O(n); Count O(n)
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for storage, O(1) for operations
